coredata.dc:title: S, N co-doped graphene quantum dots decorated TiO2 and supported with carbon for oxygen reduction reaction catalysis

coredata.dc:description: Sluggish kinetics and catalyst instability in oxygen reduction reaction are the central issues in fuel cell and metal-air battery technologies. For that, highly active, stable, and low-cost non-platinum based electrocatalysts for oxygen reduction reaction are an immediate requirement in fuel cell and metal-air battery technologies. A new composite (S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800) is synthesized, made of sulfur (S) and nitrogen (N) co-doped graphene quantum dot (GQD) with TiO2. This composite is supported on carbon on heating at 800 °C under N2 atmosphere and is explored for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) catalyst. The synthesized composite S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800, shows outstanding catalytic activity with an onset potential of 0.91 V and a half-wave potential of 0.82 V vs. RHE, an alkaline medium. The Tafel slope of the catalyst is 61 mV dec−1. The catalyst is an excellent methanol tolerant and shows good stability in an alkaline medium. The excellent ORR activity of S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 is ascribed to well-built interactivity between the S,N-GQD/TiO2, and the carbon support. The unique structure offers advantages, with outstanding electrical conductivity, high surface area, and excellent charge transfer kinetics between the doped GQD and TiO2 interface and subsequently from the carbon surface to the S,N-GQD/TiO2.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The crystallite size (D) is measured from the familiar Scherrer's equation. The formula is stated below: D = κλ/βcosθ, Where κ is the shape factor, λ is the X-ray wavelength, θ is the Bragg angle, and β is the full width at half maximum (FWHM).

head.ce:title: S, N co-doped graphene quantum dots decorated TiO2 and supported with carbon for oxygen reduction reaction catalysis

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sluggish kinetics and catalyst instability in oxygen reduction reaction are the central issues in fuel cell and metal-air battery technologies. For that, highly active, stable, and low-cost non-platinum based electrocatalysts for oxygen reduction reaction are an immediate requirement in fuel cell and metal-air battery technologies. A new composite (S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800) is synthesized, made of sulfur (S) and nitrogen (N) co-doped graphene quantum dot (GQD) with TiO2. This composite is supported on carbon on heating at 800 °C under N2 atmosphere and is explored for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) catalyst. The synthesized composite S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800, shows outstanding catalytic activity with an onset potential of 0.91 V and a half-wave potential of 0.82 V vs. RHE, an alkaline medium. The Tafel slope of the catalyst is 61 mV dec−1. The catalyst is an excellent methanol tolerant and shows good stability in an alkaline medium. The excellent ORR activity of S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 is ascribed to well-built interactivity between the S,N-GQD/TiO2, and the carbon support. The unique structure offers advantages, with outstanding electrical conductivity, high surface area, and excellent charge transfer kinetics between the doped GQD and TiO2 interface and subsequently from the carbon surface to the S,N-GQD/TiO2.

ce:section.ce:para: A fuel cell (FC) is an energy conversion tool with a high energy density (600 Wh Kg−1 and 300 Wh liter−1) [1]. In a fuel cell, electricity is produced via an electrochemical process. The only by-products in a fuel cell that uses hydrogen as the fuel would be water and heat, making it attractive for sustainable energy generation [2]. Also, fuel cells are more efficient than internal combustion engines [2,3]. They are known for their high efficiency, less emission, and zero noise pollution features, making them a promising clean energy source [4,5].

There are two types of reactions that occur in polymer electrolyte membrane fuel cell (PEMFC), one is hydrogen oxidation (Anode part), and the other one is oxygen reduction (Cathode part). The oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) is considered the primary rate-defining process in a fuel cell. ORR usually happens through either a 2 electron (2 e−) or a 4 electron (4 e−) process. The surface structure of the used electrode materials [6] and the pH of the solution [7] influences the ORR. The 4 e− transfer process in ORR is preferred for fuel cells since it usually results in the direct conversion of H2 and O2 to water (H2O) and provides high efficiency.

One of the biggest bottlenecks connected to fuel cell technology has to do with the slow reaction rate typically associated with the ORR at the cathode. Usually, to ensure sufficient ORR kinetics, catalysts comprising expensive noble metals (Pt or Pt-based alloys) are used. The carbon-supported noble metal-based catalysts (Pt/C) are most extensively tested in fuel cells [8]. Unfortunately, however, due to the carbon support's corrosive tendency, the noble metal nanoparticles aggregate into larger particles during operation. This reduces the electrochemically active surface area [9,10].

Furthermore, the high cost and shortage of precious metal catalysts severely restrict large scale applications. To overcome these problems, studies are being carried out to enable the development of non-precious metal-based catalysts. But challenges prevail, especially associated with the achievement of high enough efficiency when low cost and earth-abundant materials are tested as a catalyst.

Metal oxides (e.g.,TiO2, SnO2, and CeO2) usually offer excellent chemical steadiness and better anti-corrosion resistance [11,12]. TiO2 is well recognized for the support of noble metal catalysts. The high stability and good synergistic characteristics of TiO2 make them excellent support materials for noble metal catalysts [13,14]. Noble metal/TiO2 catalysts show advanced ORR activity compared to noble metal/C catalysts [15]. These catalysts, however, are still rather expensive and prone to poisoning. Hence it is of relevance to explore purely non-noble metal catalysts.

Recently, the composites of nanoscale carbon materials along with TiO2 have gained significant attention, particularly in materials involving graphene owing to its excellent electronic conductivity and better stability [16,17]. Generally, graphene-based catalysts are well known for their excellent stability and better selectivity towards ORR in alkaline medium [18–20]. Nanocarbon supported metal oxide for ORR has been explored [16,21–24]. Jiahao Guo et al. have found that Co3O4 nanospheres that are uniformly anchored on N doped reduced graphene oxides sheets show excellent catalytic activity for ORR. Their results show an interaction between Co3O4 nanosphere and N-rGO, which helps in electron transfer between them [25]. Likewise, Anna S. Ryabova et al. have studied the role of different carbon support in manganese oxides/carbon composites. They have seen that proper morphology, texture, and the optimized surface area of the carbon-based support are vital factors to get synergistic effect between metal oxides and carbon nanocomposites [26]. In fact Haitao Wang et al. have reported uniform Fe3O4 nanoparticles embedded in nitrogen-doped mesoporous carbon spheres for ORR [27].

Despite the promise of TiO2, electronic conductivity for the ORR process is limited due to its semiconducting behaviour (bandgap~3.2ev) [17]. In recent times, numerous efforts have been absorbed towards using TiO2 based nanocomposites with carbon-related materials, which offer the prospect of enhanced catalytic activity because of the exceptional charge transport characteristics [28]. Of relevance here is also the work of Yi Zhang et al. who have reported reduced graphene oxide supported TiO2 composites with different morphologies for ORR. They have found that TiO2/rGO (Octahedral) show excellent ORR activity due to strong coupling between metal oxide TiO2 and rGO [29]. Furthermore Shangjbin Jin et al. have reported a new fabrication approach of hybrid titanium oxides/carbon materials. They prepared titanium oxides supported on nitrogen-doped nanoporous carbon from an amorphous titanium porous coordination polymer, and found it to have significant catalytic activity [30]. Also, Ramireddy Boppella et al. have reported conductive Ti3+ self-doped carbon-coated TiO2 anchored on reduced graphene oxides hollow nanosphere. It offers excellent catalytic activity and stability for ORR [31].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are nanocarbon materials that are gaining increasing attention. The GQDs are usually below 10 nm in size [32–34]. These are emerging materials in science and technological fields. For example, GQDs find use in bioimaging, sensing, and catalysis owing to their exceptional electronic conductivity, large surface area, narrow dimension, and a lesser amount of harmfulness [35,36]. In addition, nanocarbon materials can be modified through the introduction of heteroatoms (e.g., S, B, and N) to improve catalytic activity [37–41] This is because heteroatoms can intensely change the local spin or charge distribution by bringing an irregular charge distribution in the material. This, in turn, results in enhanced electronic conductivity. This also helps in the promotion of adsorption and activation of molecular oxygen [38,42,43].

Furthermore, doping with appropriate heteroatoms enhances the number of active sites, which helps in the adsorption of O2 molecules easily and offers the possibility of decreasing the potential required for ORR [5,44]. Nitrogen-doped graphene on graphite oxide (NG/GCE) improved ORR activity compared to graphene on graphite oxide (G/GCE) [39]. The sulfur-doped graphene electrode also shows more positive onset potential than pure graphene [38]. Hence appropriately doped GQDs are prospective materials for ORR application [45]. It may be noted that heteroatom doped GQD/TiO2 composites have been explored widely in photocatalytic applications [46,47].

This is the first report where we have used doped GQD/TiO2 or, in general, doped GQD/metal oxide composites for ORR electrocatalysis. This paper presents a highly electrochemically active material, labelled S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800, for the oxygen reduction reaction. The number on the label stands for the temperature at which the heat treatment is done. For the fabrication of the composite, two steps hydrothermal technique is performed. In the first step, TiO2 nanoparticles are synthesized, and then in the next step, TiO2 nanoparticles are decorated by S,N-GQDs. Due to its simplicity and low infrastructure requirements, the method fits vast-scale production. Compared with S,N-GQD/TiO2, and S,N-GQD/TiO2/C, the S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 nanocomposite system is shown to have excellent ORR activity. The structures, morphological properties, and electrochemical characteristics of the synthesized S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800, are duly characterized.

Titanium (IV) tetraisopropoxide (Sigma Aldrich), citric acid (Rankem), thiourea (Rankem), ketjenblack carbon (KBC) (EC-600JD) (Akzo Noble), and Nafion solution (5 wt%) (Alfa Aesar) are purchased and used without further purification. Solvents of high purity are used in this study.

An easy hydrothermal synthesis technique is used to synthesize TiO2 nanoparticles, described by Laura Cano-Casanova et al., with marginal modification [48]. In this method, first titanium tetraisopropoxide (4 mL) is mixed to ethanol (20 mL) with the help of magnetic stirring (1 h) and then in an ultrasonic bath (30 min). After that, a solution containing H2O (4 mL) and ethanol (10 mL) is mixed, and the mixture is left for constant stirring (1 h). Next, the mixture is heated at 180 °C for 12 h in an autoclave. The collected powder is washed with water and ethanol. After that, it is dried at 90 °C. The sample is then heated at 350 °C for 2 h in the O2 atmosphere.

S,N-GQD/TiO2 nanocomposites are synthesized using an easy hydrothermal synthesis technique described by Mehdi Rahbar et al. with some modification [49]. In this method, initially, the citric acid (2 mmol) and thiourea (6 mmol) are adequately mixed in 20 ml DI water with magnetic stirring (20 min). Then as prepared, 0.2 g TiO2 nano-powder is integrated into the solution; this is followed by ultrasonication for 1 h. The solution mixture is heated at 180 °C for 6 h in a Teflon-lined autoclave. The composite is collected by centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 10 min. This is followed by washing with water and ethanol. Finally, it is dried at 90 °C in a vacuum oven.

To prepare the S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 sample, first as prepared S,N-GQD/TiO2 is mixed with Ketjenblack carbon in a 1:1 ratio by proper grinding. After that, the mixture is heated at 800 °C for 2 h in N2 atmosphere in a tubular furnace at the heating rate of 5 °C min−1. The S,N- GQD/TiO2-800 sample is prepared using the same heating conditions without adding carbon. All the synthesis procedure involved is well illustrated by Scheme 1 .

All the electrodes are prepared in an identical condition. The electrode preparation is as follows: 10 mg of the synthesized composites are taken in a 5 ml vial with 1000 μl mixture of water and ethanol (1:1) and ultrasonicated for 30 min. Afterward, 20 μl of Nafion solution (5 wt %) is mixed and sonicated for another 30 min. After that, 20 μl of the resultant solution is drop casted onto a glassy carbon (GC) electrode and thoroughly dried. The catalyst loading is 0.8 mg cm−2.

Morphological and elemental analyses are performed using a high-resolution scanning electron microscope (HR-SEM) (operating voltage – 500 V to 30 KV). Energy dispersive x-ray (EDAX) for x-rays generated is captured using a silicon drifts detector (SDD) X-Max (80 mm2). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and Selected area electron diffraction pattern (SAED) images are collected from Tecnai T20 TEM. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images are collected from JEOL 3010.

Crystallographic information is obtained from powder X-ray diffraction using an X-ray diffractometer (PanAlytical) with Cu Kα (λ = 1.5406 Å) at room temperature. The operational parameters for the measurement conducted are 40 kV and 30 mA for 10°–80° with a 0.02° step size. The defect levels and confirmation of the nanocomposite formation are analyzed using Confocal Raman Spectroscopy System Model: WiTEC R 300 Source: Nd-YAG, 532 nm. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy is carried out on JASCO-4100 Type A. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is carried out using Al Kα X-rays in Omicron ESCA Probe X-ray photoelectron spectrometer under an ultra-high vacuum of 10−8 Pa.

All the electrochemical characterizations have been done in a three electrodes pyrex glass system. Potassium chloride (KCl) saturated Ag/AgCl, Pt wire, and catalysts coated glassy carbon (GC) electrodes are used as the reference, counter, and working electrode, respectively. N2 and O2 saturated 0.1 M KOH solutions are taken as electrolytes independently. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) are conducted with the help of Metrohm multi-Autolab M204 (MAC90009) potentiostat/galvanostat. GC rotating ring disk electrode (RRDE) from Pine Instrument is used to assess the ORR kinetics.

The RRDE experiments are performed on 5.5 mm diameter GCE disk surrounded by Pt ring (with outward diameter 8.5 mm and inward diameter 6.5 mm). All the potential values from CVs and LSVs measurements in the alkaline electrolyte are converted to the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) via Eqs. (1) and (2), respectively.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is conducted in the identical cell setup. It is done at open-circuit voltage in the frequency range of 10 kHz to 10 Hz. The AC voltage is used to 5 mV. The ZSimpWin software fits the EIS spectra.

Comparative XRD patterns of S,N-GQDs, Ketjenblack carbon (C), pure TiO2, S,N-GQD/TiO2, S,N-GQD/TiO2/C, and S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 are presented in Fig. 1 . Fig. 1a shows a wide peak at about 26° for the S,N-GQDs, agreeing with the (002) diffraction planes of graphite carbon [50]. The broadening of the XRD peak of S,N-GQDs indicates the nano-size associated with GQDs. Fig. 1b shows XRD peaks at 25.54°, 38.11°, 48.26°, 54.70°, 62.89°, 68.97°, 70.38°, and 75.41° for all the samples. All the diffraction peaks are indexed with the TiO2 (anatase) phase (JCPDS file no. 04–0477). Due to the less amount and proper spreading of S,N-GQDs in TiO2, the TiO2 crystal structure remains the same.

The peak shift has also been observed, shown in Fig. 1c–e separately for better understanding. Nevertheless, the XRD peaks are little shifted to lesser angles after the addition of S,N-GQD, followed by heat treatment. The peak shift implies that the lattice constant of TiO2 is enhanced, and lattice distortion appears by adding the S,N-GQD, and heat treatment. It is confirmed that the appearance of S,N-GQD during the synthesis time creates surface stress in the TiO2 crystals, indicates lattice distortion.

The S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 sample has the maximum amount of lattice distortion among all the samples (Table 1 ). The lattice distortion enhances the making of an oxygen vacancy within the TiO2 structure during heat treatment at 800 °C [51]. Because of the lattice distortion, the bond length of S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 is increased, which energetically supports creating oxygen vacancies in TiO2 owing to lesser binding energy [52]. The crystallite sizes (10–13 nm) determined using the Debye Scherer formula are by and large the same across all the samples [53]. Still, it is noticeable that the crystallite size has increased due to heat treatment compared to unheated samples, as presented in Table 1. The d spacing of the TiO2 (101) plane is found to be around 0.35 nm through Braggs law from the XRD data.

Fig. 2 a shows FT-IR analysis of S,N-GQDs, TiO2, S,N-GQDs/TiO2, and S,N-GQDs/TiO2/C-800 samples. In S,N-GQDs, a broad absorption band is observed at around 3000-3500 cm−1, indicating the amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface of S,N-GQDs [49]. The peaks at 1621, 1405, 1195, and 607 cm−1 are assigned to CN, C–OH, C–H, C–O, and C–S stretching vibration, respectively [49,54]. The FTIR spectrum of the pure TiO2 shows O–H stretching and bending vibration peaks at 3432 and 1631 cm−1. Also, the FTIR of pure TiO2 nanoparticles has a broad peak at 400–800 cm−1 corresponding to Ti–O and Ti–O–Ti [54].

Fig. 2a also shows the FTIR spectrum of the S,N-GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite, which is characterized by C–O stretching (1398 cm−1), O–H stretching, and bending vibration (3460 and 1631 cm−1), which indicates the successful formation of S,N-GQD on TiO2 nanoparticles [49,54]. The characteristics (Ti–O–Ti stretching mode) of the TiO2 are observed in all the nanocomposites. It has been observed that the main feature, i.e., Ti–O–Ti infrared absorption decreases in the nanocomposites case compared to pure TiO2, thus indicating the presence of oxygen vacancies [55,56]. We also see a slight red shift of the Ti–O–Ti absorption in the composite samples that correspond to the pure TiO2, indicating successful formation S,N-GQD on TiO2 through Ti–O–C formation [57]. In S,N-GQDs/TiO2/C-800 sample Ti–O–Ti infrared absorption is significantly decreased at 400–800 cm−1 compared to S,N-GQDs/TiO2, and S,N-GQDs/TiO2/C. This result indicates that the number of oxygen vacancies in the S,N-GQDs/TiO2/C-800 are more [56].

Fig. 2b shows the Raman spectrum of TiO2, S,N-GQD/TiO2, S,N-GQD/TiO2/C, and S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800. All the samples display similar characteristic Raman bands of anatase TiO2 [17]. Five typical peaks are positioned at 143, 197, 399,517, and 639.9 cm−1, corresponding to anatase TiO2 [17,28]. In S,N-GQD/TiO2, G peak is observed at 1513 cm−1. The defect peak D is not detectable in S,N-GQD/TiO2, which indicates a rather low defect concentration in this sample. A broad peak is detected at about 2500–2800 cm−1, indicating the graphene's 2D peak. This results suggest, the graphene constituent in the S,N-GQD/TiO2 is a few atomic layers thick [58].

For other samples, such as S,N-GQD/TiO2/C, and S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800, the D and G peaks are positioned at 1335 and 1581 cm−1, respectively. Besides, the 2D peak intensity has reduced in S,N-GQD/TiO2/C and S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 sample, respectively, when compared to S,N-GQD/TiO2. This indicates more defects than S,N-GQD/TiO2, or we may infer that the number of layers (hence thickness) of graphene constituent increases in S,N-GQD/TiO2/C, and S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 [59]. It is also noted that the intensities of the TiO2 peaks are reduced in all the composites samples when compared to pure TiO2. Along with a blue shift of Eg (1), Raman mode of pure TiO2 has been observed in the composite samples (Fig. 2c and Table 2 ). This indicates chemical and/or physical connections between the S,N-GQD, and TiO2 [59,60]. Hence, we conclude that S,N-GQD are possibly physically and/or chemically attached to the TiO2 particles' surface.

A blue shift of Eg (1) Raman mode, accompanied by peak broadening in S,N-GQD/TiO2, S,N-GQD/TiO2/C, and S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 samples, is attributable to the oxygen vacancies [61]. Among all the samples S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 has gained maximum oxygen vacancies, which helps to get better catalytic activity [17]. This suggests that oxygen defect concentrations increase after heating of the samples; this, in turn, correlates with the observed lattice distortion seen in XRD results (Table 1). It reflects well-built interactivity between doped graphene quantum dot and TiO2 via oxygen vacancy in TiO2. The highest peak shift in the S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 is plausibly because of the proper interaction of S,N-GQD, and TiO2, forming C–O–Ti structure through the generated oxygen vacancies [17].

Also, we see a defective carbon structure from the ID/IG value. Among all the sample S,N-GQDs/TiO2/C-800 has the highest ID/IG = 1.05 value and indicates a more defective structure. G band is related to the E2g mode of the sp2carbon domain, and the D band is for the structural imperfection of the sp2 region. The ratio (ID/IG = 1.05) recommends that S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 holds rich surface defects since it has been reported that for the highest quality of graphene, the ID/IG ratio would be smaller than 1 [62,63].

Fig. 3 a and b represent the HR-SEM micrographs of the S,N-GQD/TiO2 composite at various magnifications. The images indicate that the size of the TiO2 nanoparticles is almost identical with a marginal agglomeration and the particles look like sphere shape. Fig. 3c shows a micrograph of S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800, which defines that the TiO2 nanoparticles are distributed well on the carbon surface. The size distribution of TiO2 nanoparticles is presented in Fig. 3d. The nanoparticle's size is 13.3
±
2.3 nm, nearly the same as a result evaluated using XRD (Table 1).

To understand elemental analysis of S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 composite, EDAX measurements have been done. Fig. 4 a–h shows the SEM image, EDAX spectra, and elemental analysis of S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 composite. EDAX results in Fig. 4b show the existence of constituent elements. Fig. 4c shows the carbon elements' homogenous dispersion, indicating that the S,N-GQDs are rather uniformly spread over the TiO2 surface. Importantly, N and S signals are overlaid with the primary C signal, Ti signal, and O signal. Therefore, it can be claimed that the graphene quantum dots spread in the composite is doped by the N and S elements. Fig. S2 also shows the details of EDAX mapping and elemental analysis for S,N-GQD/TiO2. We can see similar characteristics in S,N-GQD/TiO2 as S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800, except the distribution of carbon element is less in the S,N-GQD/TiO2. Because in the S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800, the distribution of carbon element is more because of the carbon support.

The TEM micrographs in Fig. 5 are represented to gain more information about the morphology of S,N-GQD/TiO2, and S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800. Fig. 5a represents the TEM image of S,N-GQD/TiO2, which indicates the nearly monodisperse small sphere-shaped black dots of S,N-GQDs with TiO2 nanoparticles. TEM images obtained with different resolutions have been presented in supporting information Fig. S1a-b. Fig. 5b and c indicates S,N-GQDs/TiO2 particles' uniform distribution over the carbon supports. Fig. 5d shows the TEM image of as-prepared S,N-GQDs. It can be seen that the S,N-GQDs are dispersed uniformly without clear aggregations. The HR-TEM image is shown in the inset of Fig. 5d. The lattice spacing is about 0.25 nm due to the in-plane lattice spacing of graphene (100 facet) [49]. UV–visible absorption spectrum of S,N-GQDs is shown in Fig. S1c. It shows a typical absorption peak at 328 nm, assigns to n-π∗ transition. Another absorption hump is observed at 400 nm, indicating the π-π∗ transition [64].

Fig. 5e shows the HR-TEM image of S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 with a lattice spacing of 0.25 and 0.39 nm of S,N-GQDs and TiO2 in it, respectively. Fig. 5f shows the SAED image of S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 where lattice spacings of 0.37 nm and 0.244 nm are allotted to (101) planes of anatase TiO2 (JCPDS file no. 01–0562) and (100) planes of graphitic carbon (JCPDS file no. 08–0415), respectively [49]. Fig. 5g and h shows particle size distribution curve for S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 where an average size of the S,N-GQDs is 3.75 ± 0.90 nm and for TiO2 is 12.75
±
1.7
nm measured using the TEM images, which is consistent with SEM (Fig. 3d) and XRD results (Table 1).

To determine the chemical composition and surface state in the different samples, XPS measurement is conducted. The full spectrum of the S,N-GQD/TiO2 S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 are shown in Fig. 6 a, indicates that the composites are composed of carbon (C1s) (284 eV), nitrogen (N1s) (397 eV), sulfur (S2p) (169 eV), titanium (Ti 2p) (460 eV), and oxygen (O1s) (530 eV) [44]. We have observed weak N1s and S2p signal because of less doping. There are intensities differences in the carbon peaks after adding the carbon support in S,N-GQD/TiO2, shown in Fig. 6a.

The high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s of S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 shows four peaks i.e., C–C (284.7 eV), C–O/C–N/C–S (286.05 eV), CO(287 eV), and O–CO (289.9 eV) in Fig. 6b [17,65]. Along with C–N or C–S bond, oxygen-rich groups like CO, O–CO, and C–O are also confirmed [65]. Noted that the Ti–C related peak in C1s is absent, suggesting Ti–C is bonded through oxygen atoms to form the possible C–O–Ti bond formation, which is also confirmed by Raman (Fig. 2b) and FTIR (Fig. 2a.) results [17].

The surface state in the different catalysts is studied from O1s XPS spectra's core level. We can see that for all three samples, we have got three deconvoluted peaks in O1s, corresponds to Ti–O (529.7 eV), C–O (531.4 eV), and C–OH (533 eV) [59,66]. In the O1s spectra in Fig. 6d–f, C–O peak is used to define oxygen vacancy formation in TiO2, forming C–O–Ti structure formation in the composites [17,55]. The C–O peak in O1s is considered a peak associated with oxygen vacancy formation. For all the samples, oxygen vacancies are present, which helps ORR catalytic activity [17,67,68].

To compare the oxygen vacancy concentration, we have taken the area ratio of the C–O peak to the total area of O1s peak for each sample [69]. It has been observed that oxygen vacancy concentration in S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 (25%) is higher than S,N-GQD/TiO2 (21%), and S, N-GQD/TiO2/C (23%), indicates higher ORR activity [67,68].

Oxygen vacancies formation also confirm from the high-resolution XPS data of Ti 2p (Fig. 6c). In Fig. 6c the Ti 2p peaks are cantered at 459.5 eV (Ti 2p3/2) and 465.3 eV (Ti 2p1/2), which are belongs to the of Ti4+ state [51,70]. The shoulder peaks are also detected at lower binding energies for the sample, which can be ascribed to Ti3+ ions [51,70]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 6g) shows three peaks, which are defined as a pyrrolic N (399.4 eV), graphitic N (402.2 eV), and nitrogen oxide (N-ox) (405.2 eV) [66,71]. Besides, in S2p (Fig. 6h), we can see the presence of S2p3/2 and S2p1/2 position of C–S bond. This result indicates that sulfur is present in the form of thiopine like structure [66].

Different configurations of nitrogen (N) atoms present in the carbon network affect the catalytic activity due to the change of carbon atoms' electronic structures. It has been reported that the graphitic N configuration is responsible for limiting current density, while both the pyridinic and pyrrolic N configurations are responsible for onset potential for ORR [72,73]. The sulfur-doped further modifies the long-range interaction between S and N sites [73].

High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, N1s, and S2p of S,N-GQD/TiO2 sample are shown in Fig. S3. In both S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 and S,N-GQD/TiO2 samples, the characteristics of the XPS spectra (C1s, N1s, and S2p) are found to similar. The amount of carbon element is more in S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 sample compared to S,N-GQD/TiO2 sample due to carbon support, which is also shown in XPS survey spectra (Fig. 6a). The C1s spectrum is fitted with four peaks for both the samples S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 (Fig. 6b) and S,N-GQD/TiO2 (Fig. S3a). It has been observed that the relative percentage of C–C peak of S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 is 61%, whereas it is 48% for S,N-GQD/TiO2. The relative percentages are calculated taking ratio between the corresponding peak area to the total peak area. Higher percentage of C–C peak in S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 is due to the presence of extra carbon support. In addition, the other three peaks are CO, O–CO, and C–O/C–N/C–S, which are assigned as oxygen related functional groups in S,N-GQDs for both S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 and S,N-GQD/TiO2 samples.

It is evident from the fitted data that these oxygen-related functional groups are reduced in S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 (Fig. 6b) compare to S,N-GQD/TiO2 (Fig. S3a). These can be attributed due to the presence of carbon support and heat treatment in S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800. Also, the relative percentages of different N contents in N1s for both the samples S,N-GQD/TiO2 and S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 have been calculated. It has been observed that the relative percentage of pyrrolic N (37.5%) and graphitic N (34%) are more in S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 compare to S,N-GQD/TiO2 (pyrrolic N(34%) and graphitic N(32%)) which is known to aid in improving catalytic activity [72,73]. The elemental quantification of S,N-GQD/TiO2 is shown in Table S1.

To test the electrochemical performance of the synthesized materials, a three electrodes system is used. Fig. 7 a presents the CVs of S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 catalyst in 0.1 M KOH. In S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800, a sharp reduction peak is appeared at 0.91 V vs. RHE in O2 saturated condition. On the other hand, no peak is appeared in the N2 saturated condition, indicating O2 is reduced on the electrode. Fig. 7b compares the CVs behaviour of S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800, with S,N-GQD/TiO2, S,N-GQD/TiO2/C, KBC, and 20% Pt/C (obtained from Sigma Aldrich) catalysts in O2 saturated condition. The cathodic peak potential is appeared at 0.76 V for S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800, whereas S,N-GQD/TiO2/C, KBC, and Pt/C show the potential at 0.78, 0.69, and 0.89 V vs. RHE. CV curve of S,N-GQD/TiO2 has been plotted separately to visible correctly in Fig. S4e showing peak potential at 0.55 V vs. RHE.

The peak current density of −3.69 mA cm−2 is observed for S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 catalyst whereas, S,N-GQD/TiO2/C catalyst produces a peak current density of - 3.068 mA cm−2.Though S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 shows less peak potential than the Pt/C (0.89 V), the peak current density is better than that in Pt/C (−1.6 mA cm−2).

The above results suggest that the S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 composite displays enhanced ORR activity related to others. The improved reduction current may be because of the proper interaction between S,N-GQD/TiO2, and carbon, along with sufficient oxygen vacancies formation during heat treatment, which has been verified using XPS, Raman, and FTIR data. In addition to proper dispersion, coupled with less agglomeration of S,N-GQD/TiO2 nanoparticles on carbon make this system a good candidate for enhancing electrocatalytic activity. In addition, carbon is also used to offer an excessive surface area with high electronic conductivity.

Fig. 7d shows the rotation rate-dependent LSV graph of S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 in O2 saturated condition. The results show that limiting current density is directly proportional to the rotation rates, reflecting diffusion-limited ORR activity. Fig. 7c compares the LSVs data of the different samples at 1600 rpm. The onset potential for S,N-GQD/TiO2, and S,N-GQD/TiO2/C catalysts are observed at 0.68 V and 0.89 V, whereas for S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 catalyst, the onset potential is 0.91 V. Also, we see that KBC alone is less catalytically active. On the other-hand, Pt/C is showing, as usual, higher onset potential. The half-wave potential of the S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 composite is 0.82 V vs. RHE, which is better than S,N-GQD/TiO2 (0.65 V), and S,N-GQD/TiO2/C (0.80 V) catalysts.

To verify the exact origin of the catalytic activity, we have performed an analysis of various control samples (Fig. S4d). The LSV graph shows that the catalytic activity is mainly because of the good synergistic effect of S,N-GQD/TiO2, and carbon support. Along with the heat treatment of this sample is also helpful to increase the limiting current density forming plenty of oxygen vacancies. The substantial increase of limiting current density in S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 compared to S,N-GQD/TiO2/C is due to heat treatment. It is also proven by comparing the ORR performance of S,N-GQD/TiO2 and S,N-GQD/TiO2-800 samples which is shown in Fig. S4f. Again TiO2/C sample is not that active compare to S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800, and S,N-GQD/TiO2/C which is shown in Fig. S4d. This result indicates synergistic effect between TiO2 and carbon is not up to the mark. The electrochemical performance of all the catalysts is listed in Table S2. Fig. S4a, S4b, and S4c also show the rotation rate-dependent LSV graphs of S,N-GQD/TiO2, S,N-GQD/TiO2-800, and S,N-GQD/TiO2/C, respectively, indicate the same diffusion-limited ORR activity. S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 is compared with existing literature, shows it is one of the best materials in catalytic activity among different metal oxide carbon nanocomposites (Table S3).

From this observation, we can say that carbon support plays the most important role in getting high catalytic activity. Due to proper interaction between S,N-GQD/TiO2, and carbon support, the synergistic effect is favorable for the excellent catalytic activity. Along with heat treatment at 800 °C of the composite, it may create more active sites and oxygen vacancies in the materials [67,68]. As a result, a large number of O2 molecules could be adsorbed on the catalyst surface resulting in a higher diffusion-limited current density. Moreover, the crystal lattice of TiO2 is distorted to some extent due to the doped GQD. The TiO2 lattice distortion has been confirmed using XRD (Fig. 1c–e) and Raman results (Fig. 2b). The distorted crystal lattice and vacancies also tend to make the O2 reduction reaction process more straightforward in the S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 catalyst [17]. Finally, due to this effect, GQD-TiO2 form heterojunctions, which enable electron transport from GQD into TiO2 and subsequently to carbon sheet [60,74].

The number of electrons (n) associated with the ORR is evaluated from the Koutecky–Levich (K–L) analysis shown in Fig. 8 a. K–L Eqs. (3)–(6) are used to estimate the n.

The n value for S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 sample, is 3.6, demonstrating that the ORR happens through the 4 e− process, which is considered good for obtaining desirable kinetics. The K-L plots for the various samples are also shown in Fig. S5. It has been observed that the n values are 1.78, 2.64, 2.75 for S,N-GQD/TiO2, KBC (C), and S,N-GQD/TiO2/C respectively. The different number of electron transfers for the different samples are responsible for the different catalytic activity.

Fig. 8b represents the Tafel plots from the LSV of 1600 rpm using Eq. (4) for the catalysts. The excellent ORR kinetics for S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 catalyst is observed, reflecting the smaller Tafel slope of 61 mV dec−1 compared to S,N-GQD/TiO2 (66 mV dec−1), and S,N-GQD/TiO2/C (62 mV dec−1).

A high activity can be obtained for carbon-based materials at heating temperatures between 500 °C and 800 °C in an inert environment [75,76]. In this temperature zone, a thermal rearrangement of the carbon, nitrogen, sulfur, and metal groups creates active ORR sites. It has also been observed that above 800 °C, the formation of metallic particles or metal oxide/carbide occurs with a decrease in nitrogen content, leading to reduced activity [76]. In previous studies, it has been reported that 700–800 °C is the optimized temperature to get the best catalytic activity [75,76]. Hence our study indicates that heating to 800 °C helps in achieving good catalytic activity.

The RRDE experiment is done for S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 catalyst coated electrode in which the disk is exposed to the LSV and Pt ring to chronoamperometry at 1.23 V vs. RHE. The long-term durability test of S,N-QD/TiO2/C-800 catalyst is conducted by a standard accelerated durability test (ADT). It is done in the potential range of 0.1–1.1 V vs. RHE. The LSVs are recorded at 5 mV s−1 at each interval of 500 cycles of CVs. Fig. 9 a shows the LSV plot of S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 catalyst taken after multiple cycles of CVs, which compares the LSV data from initial to 2000 cycles at the interval of each 500 cycles. There are only 65 mV shifts in half-wave potential (E 1/2) for S,N-QD/TiO2/C-800 catalyst, even after 2000 cycles of CVs, as shown in Fig. 9b.

The desirable ORR performance of the synthesized catalyst can be attributed to sulfur and nitrogen co-doping. The nitrogen and sulfur doping introduces asymmetry in charge and spin density. The unbalanced charge distribution due to nitrogen and sulfur co-doping is favorable to oxygen adsorption. On the other hand, strong coupling interaction between S,N-GQD/TiO2, and carbon support can facilitate electron transfer and provide good stability.

To test the stability of the catalyst, a chronoamperometric test is performed for 15 h in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH at 0.55 V at 800 rpm. Fig. 9c shows the retention in relative current density with time. It has been observed that the S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 shows an 80% current retention, which is relatively closer to the standard Pt/C catalyst with 84% current retention, suggesting good stability in 0.1 M KOH.

Methanol tolerance property of the S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 catalyst is investigated. For the test, 1 M aqueous methanol solution is used as the electrolyte. LSV is performed at the rotation of 800 rpm and 5 mV s−1. The response shows that the catalyst is highly methanol tolerant, as shown in Fig. 9d. This study is compared with a commercial Pt/C catalyst. In Pt/C, there is a typical large oxidation peak observed; however, there is no such peak in the case of S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 catalyst. It indicates the S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 catalyst is highly methanol tolerant.

EIS analysis is performed to know the charge transfer kinetics for the ORR. All the Nyquist plots display a semicircle (High-frequency region) with a straight line (Low-frequency region). These are associated with the interface charge transfer process and diffusion process [77].

The EIS results suggests that the improved performance of S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 for ORR primarily comes from the synergistic effect between S,N-GQD/TiO2, and the carbon support. The surface resistance (Rf) and Warburg impedance (Zw) also show the lowest value in S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 compared to S,N-GQD/TiO2, and S,N-GQD/TiO2/C, which is shown in Table 3. This suggests that gas molecules' diffusion is better in S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 [77].

A new composite (S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800) is effectively synthesized using an easy hydrothermal technique. XRD characterization confirms the faceted nature of TiO2 and composites. SEM, TEM, FTIR, XPS, and Raman characterizations confirmed nanocomposites formation. Electrochemical testing is carried out for all the catalysts. The S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 shows an improved onset potential of 0.91 V and a higher half-wave potential of 0.82 V vs. RHE with a Tafel slope of 61 mV dec−1. This reflects excellent catalytic activity in an alkaline medium. The RRDE based experiment confirms that the oxygen reduction involves a 4 e− process, and the percentage of HO2 − intermediate formed is less than 20%. The S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 catalyst has only a 65 mV shift in half-wave potential even after 2000 cycles of CVs and 80% retention in relative current density for 15 h of stability test. The synergetic effects between the S,N-GQD/TiO2, and carbon in S,N-GQD/TiO2/C-800 composite results in the highest ORR activity. In addition, the heterojunction formed between doped GQD and TiO2 adds activity. This is the first report that uses GQDs in conjunction with TiO2 for ORR catalysis. It offers an opportunity for further work into this class of materials towards electrocatalytic applications.
